title: Array Types
description: Store lists of data in TypeScript.

slides.title: Lists of Data 📚
slides.content:
Arrays allow you to store multiple values in a single variable.

In pure JS, an array can hold anything: numbers, strings, and objects all mixed together. In TypeScript, we prefer arrays to hold only **one specific type** of data.

slides.title: Array Syntax
slides.content: To type an array, write the type of data it holds, followed immediately by square brackets `[]`.
slides.codeSnippet:
let highScores: number[] = [99, 85, 100];
let players: string[] = ["Alice", "Bob"];

quizData.question: Which of these correctly types an array that will strictly hold boolean values?
quizData.explanation: The type `boolean[]` indicates an array of booleans.

slides.title: The Alternative Syntax 🧬
slides.content: There is a second way to define arrays using the `Array<type>` generic syntax. It does exactly the same thing as `type[]`.

slides.title: Type Inference
slides.content: If you initialize an array immediately, e.g., `let names = ['John', 'Jane'];`, TypeScript intelligently infers that this is a `string[]` automatically without needing explicit annotation!
